steps.m: upon you (object of نَزَّلْنَا - we sent down)
steps.n: Added كَ to show who is being acted upon. This word connects to نَزَّلْنَا (we sent down) by showing that you are the one upon whom the Quran was sent down.

steps.m: to read or recite something out loud

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters together

steps.m: recitation, that which is read abundantly
steps.n: Changes verb 'read' into noun meaning 'that which is read/recited frequently'

steps.m: the Quran (object of نَزَّلْنَا/We sent down)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and َ to show it's receiving the action of being sent down
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: act of sending down (related to نَزَّلْنَا)
steps.n: Changes from a basic verb (he sent down) to a noun meaning 'sending down repeatedly' - related to نَزَّلْنَا in the same verse
The ًا ending shows this word emphasizes how the Quran was sent down, reinforcing the main verb نَزَّلْنَا (We sent down)

steps.m: to be patient or endure difficulties

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed from 'he was patient' to a command meaning 'be patient!' by adding اِ at the start and changing the vowels. Like telling someone 'Have patience!'

steps.m: so be patient! (connected to لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ - for the judgment of your Lord)
steps.n: Added فَ at the start to connect this command with what comes after it. This shows you should be patient specifically for Allah's judgment.
Root meaning 'judge' becomes noun 'judgment' by adding vowel marks

steps.m: for/to the judgment (of رَبِّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to mean 'for/to' and ِ at the end because it's connected to رَبِّكَ (your Lord) in the ayah
The root ر-ب-ب becomes 'my Lord' when you add the kasra (ِ) at the end, showing it belongs to someone or something
The كَ at the end means 'your' (when speaking to one male). This word belongs to حُكْمِ (judgment) in the phrase 'judgment of your Lord'
This is the basic negation word that tells us not to do something. Here it's telling us not to obey.
Added و at the beginning to connect this command with the previous one (فَٱصْبِرْ - be patient). It's like saying 'be patient AND do not obey'.
Added fatha on lam and alif at the end to complete the standard spelling of this negation particle.

steps.m: to follow willingly or submit to someone's command

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense and second person (you) by adding تُ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: do not obey! (connected to وَلَا before it)
steps.n: Added sukun at end to show it's a command not to do something. This comes after وَلَا (and not) to mean 'do not obey!'
Added sukun to show the word ends here
Added هُمْ at the end to show who is being referred to

steps.m: to commit a sin or wrongdoing

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to sin' to 'one who sins'

steps.m: a sinful one (object of تُطِعْ - do not obey)
steps.n: Added tanween (ًا) to show this is indefinite and is the object being acted upon. In this ayah, Allah commands not to obey such a person.
This is a connecting word that gives a choice between two things. In this ayah, it connects two types of people not to obey: ءَاثِمًا (sinful one) or كَفُورًا (ungrateful one).
Added fatha (َ) on أ and sukun (ْ) on و to show how to pronounce it correctly
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who constantly rejects/is very ungrateful
steps.n: Changed the vowel pattern and added و to show this describes someone who does this action a lot

steps.m: a very ungrateful person (object of تُطِعْ - do not obey)
steps.n: Added tanween alif (ًا) to show this describes who or what is being acted upon. In this verse, it's telling us not to obey this type of person.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changes to a command by adding ٱ at the start and changing vowels - tells someone to remember/mention
Adds kasra at the end to show it's connected to what comes after it
Adding وَ connects this command to what came before it
The root letters show the basic idea of elevation or highness
Root letters got vowels (kasra and sukun) to change from 'being high' to 'something high'
The weak letter و drops at the end, and the word takes its common meaning of 'name' (because names elevate and identify things)
Added alif at start and fatha at end to show this is the object of 'remember' (ٱذْكُرِ) in 'remember the name of your Lord'

steps.m: Lord (one who nurtures and cares for all)
steps.n: The doubled ب (shown by ّ) makes this word mean someone who does this action perfectly and completely. When used for Allah, it means He is the ultimate caretaker and nurturer of everything.
The ِ shows this word is owned by or connected to something else. Here it's connected to the word اسم (name) that comes before it.
Adding كَ shows this is your Lord. This whole phrase اسم ربك means 'name of your Lord'.
Changes the root letters into a noun meaning 'early morning' by adding vowel marks and a round 'ta' at the end

steps.m: in the early morning (connected to وَأَصِيلًا - and in the evening)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha to show this describes when something happens. This word pairs with وَأَصِيلًا (and in the evening) to show doing something throughout the day.
The ي makes it an adjective describing something with this quality
The ًا shows this word is being used as an object in the sentence

steps.m: and in the evening (connected to بُكْرَةً meaning 'morning')
steps.n: Added و meaning 'and' to connect this with the word بُكْرَةً (morning) - together they mean 'morning and evening'
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from (connects to ٱلَّيْلِ - the night)
The و connects this phrase to what came before it
The َ (fatha) at the end shows this connects to the next word (ٱلَّيْلِ - the night) and adds the meaning of 'the'
Added 'the' (ال) at the start, which causes a shadda on the first letter. This word is connected to مِنَ (from) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changes to command form by adding اُ at start and changing vowels. The sukun at the end shows it's a command.
Adds فَ meaning 'so/then' and changes اُ to ٱ because it comes after فَ
Basic preposition showing direction or purpose
The هُ means 'Him' (referring to Allah). This word shows who we should prostrate to in the command فَٱسْجُدْ (prostrate).

steps.m: to glorify, praise by declaring perfection

steps.n: Adding short vowels (fat-ha and kasra) to the root letters to make a basic verb meaning 'glorify'
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger - showing deep, complete glorification
Adding sukoon at the end makes this a command form - telling someone to do the glorification
Added هُ meaning 'Him' to show who is being glorified (referring to Allah in this context)
Added وَ (and) to connect this command with the previous command in the verse (فَٱسْجُدْ - so prostrate)
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to form the basic noun
Adding ًا at the end changes this into an adverb showing when something happens. This word tells us when the glorifying (سَبِّحْهُ) should take place.

steps.m: to extend in length or time

steps.n: Added vowels and ي to make it into an adjective meaning 'long'

steps.m: for a long time (describes سَبِّحْهُ - glorify Him)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to make it work as a time description - tells us to do the glorifying for a long time
This is the basic form of the word that means 'if' or 'whether'
Adding the shadda changes this from 'if' to a word that emphasizes what comes after it. Here it emphasizes 'these people' (هٰٓؤُلَآءِ)
Basic demonstrative word used to point to something
The alif is written as a small mark above the ه to show it's a demonstrative word
Adding ؤُ changes it from singular to plural
Adding لَآ specifies that we're pointing to something nearby
Adding ءِ completes the word. This word points to the people who are the subject of يُحِبُّونَ (they love) in this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The middle letter gets doubled (shown by shadda) because this root expresses strong emotion
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they love intensely (referring to the disbelievers in هٰٓؤُلَآءِ)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This refers back to 'these people' (هٰٓؤُلَآءِ) mentioned earlier in the verse.

steps.m: to rush or do something quickly
steps.n: This pattern makes it a noun meaning the animal
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed from a verb (doing something) to describing word showing who/what is hasty
Makes the word feminine - describes a feminine person/thing that is hasty

steps.m: the worldly life (object of يُحِبُّونَ - they love)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and fatha at the end because it's the object of the verb 'they love' in this ayah
Added conjunction و at the beginning to mean 'and'
Connected the root letters together with fatha vowels to make it a past tense verb
Added وَ (and) at start, changed to present tense by adding يَ and changing last vowel to ُ

steps.m: and they leave behind (subject of يُحِبُّونَ - they love)
steps.n: Added ونَ to show multiple people doing the action. This matches the plural subject of يُحِبُّونَ (they love) in the ayah.
Changed regular alif to long alif (آ) for proper spelling
Added fatha before attaching pronoun
Added هُمْ to show this means 'behind them'
The middle letter و is weak but stays because it's essential to the meaning
Connected the letters together and added vowel marks to make the basic noun 'day'

steps.m: a day (object of يَذَرُونَ - they leave)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this is the object being left behind in the phrase 'they leave a day'
This turns the concept of heaviness into a noun
Added ي between second and third root letters to show this is a strong quality. The word describes يَوْمًا (day) in the ayah, indicating a very difficult or burdensome day.
Added tanween with alif (ًا) to show this describes يَوْمًا (day) in the ayah. This ending shows it's an adjective describing the day.
This is a standalone pronoun used to say 'we'. It doesn't come from a root.

steps.m: indeed we (subject of خَلَقْنٰهُمْ/we created them)
steps.n: Adding emphasis to show this is definitely or surely true. This word is connected to خَلَقْنٰهُمْ (we created them) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Adding a sukun (circle mark) on the last letter shows we are pausing here
Added نٰ to show 'we' did the action. This completes the meaning started by نَّحْنُ (we) in the ayah.
Added هُمْ to show who was created
Connected the root letters together with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Added sukun on final letter to prepare for attachment of نا
Added نا to show 'we' did the action
Changed alif to alif madd for pronunciation
Added و at beginning to connect with previous phrase
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and form a noun meaning the result of binding/strengthening

steps.m: joint, binding, or strength (object of شَدَدْنَا)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end because this word is the object being acted upon by شَدَدْنَا (We strengthened)
Added هُمْ at the end to show these joints/strength belong to them. This whole phrase is being acted upon by شَدَدْنَا (We strengthened)
This is a conditional particle that shows when something happens. Here it connects to the next word شِئْنَا (we will) to show when that action will occur.

steps.m: and when (connecting to شِئْنَا - we will)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it. The و here connects this new condition to the previous statements about creation.
Added fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb. The middle letter ي changes to ا in this pattern.
Added نَا at the end and changed the middle of the word to show 'we' did the action in the past
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Adding shadda on middle letter makes the meaning stronger - showing a complete or thorough change
Added sukun to prepare for adding 'we' at the end
Added نَآ at the end to show 'we' did the action
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from a past tense verb (he resembled) to a noun meaning 'similar to' by changing the vowels
Forms broken plural pattern أَفْعَال showing multiple examples or similars
Adding fatha at the end shows this word is in the accusative case
Added pronoun هُمْ meaning 'their' to show possession

steps.m: to exchange or replace something with something else
steps.n: Verbal noun form showing the act of changing
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun showing the action itself by adding تَ at start and ي in middle

steps.m: a complete change/replacement (emphasizing the action of بَدَّلْنَا)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this word emphasizes the action of the verb بَدَّلْنَا (we replaced) in this ayah

steps.m: indeed/verily (emphasizes هٰذِهِ تَذْكِرَةٌ - 'this is a reminder')
steps.n: Adding a shadda (doubled letter) to the ن changes this from a conditional word to an emphasis word. It makes what comes after it more certain and important.
Basic demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this' for masculine objects
Added هٰ at the beginning to make it more specific and emphatic, like saying 'this right here'
Changed the ending to make it feminine, like changing 'this' to refer to a feminine object
Added a small ي (ۦ) at the end to help with pronunciation. This word points to تَذْكِرَةٌ (reminder) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Added تَ at start and ة at end, changed middle vowels to make a noun meaning 'reminder' or 'something that reminds'

steps.m: a reminder (that is being described in هٰذِهِۦ)
steps.n: Added tanween dammah to show this is indefinite and is being described by هٰذِهِۦ (this) in the ayah
This is a question word that can mean 'who' or 'whoever' depending on context. Here it's used to mean 'whoever' since it's introducing a conditional statement.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or consequence of what came before. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb شَآءَ (willed/chose) in this ayah.
Added fatha marks to connect the letters and show past tense
The middle letter changes to a long alif to show this is a hollow verb
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show past tense
The ت combines with أ to make ٱتّ, showing this is done with more purpose
Basic preposition showing direction
Added the elongated alif (ىٰ) at the end to complete the preposition. This word connects to رَبِّهِۦ (his Lord) to show movement or direction towards something.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
The middle letter ب gets doubled with shadda to show doing something a lot or with intensity
The kasra shows this word is being possessed by or connected to something after it
Adds 'his' with a prolongation mark. The word follows إِلَىٰ (towards) in this ayah, showing movement or direction towards his Lord.

steps.m: to make a way or path

steps.n: Adds vowel marks to show this is describing a path or way
Added ي (yaa) to make it a proper noun meaning permanent/established path
Added tanwin alif to show this is the object being taken in the phrase 'took a path to his Lord'
Basic negative particle used to negate the verb تَشَآءُونَ (you will). In this context, it means 'not' rather than 'what'.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. The و means 'and'.
Added fatha vowels and the weak letter ي changed to ا, making the past tense form
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' and changed the ending vowel to ُ to show present tense

steps.m: you all want/wish (connected to إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ - except that Allah wills)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people, and lengthened the ا with آ. This verb is part of a larger phrase showing that your wanting depends on Allah's will.
This is the basic form of the word that means 'except'
Adding shadda (ّ) on the ل makes the word stronger and more emphatic

steps.m: except (connecting to أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ - that Allah wills)
steps.n: The alif is lengthened to show connection to what follows. This word introduces an exception to وَمَا تَشَآءُونَ (and you do not will) by saying 'except that Allah wills'

This is a particle that connects two parts of the sentence, showing that the second part depends on or follows from the first part. In this ayah, it connects 'you will' (تَشَآءُونَ) with 'Allah wills' (يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ), showing that your will depends on Allah's will.
The weak letter ي changes to ا (alif) and the hamza أ moves to the end. Added fatha marks to show past tense.

steps.m: he wills/wishes (in relation to Allah's will)
steps.n: Added يَ at the beginning to show present tense. The alif is written long (آ) for emphasis in recitation. This word is part of a phrase about Allah's will.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
This is the unique name for The One True God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the ُ shows this is the subject of the sentence.
This is the basic form of the word that introduces a condition or statement
Adding shadda changes this from a conditional word to an emphatic particle. It makes the statement stronger and more certain. In this ayah, it emphasizes the statement about Allah.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. The hamza is dropped and the first ل gets a shadda.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of يَشَآءَ - that which Allah wills)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being acted upon. The alif at the start changed to a wasl form (ٱ) because it connects with the previous word.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters

steps.m: he was (describing Allah's attributes عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas in past tense. This word connects Allah with the attributes 'All-Knowing and All-Wise' in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Changed the pattern to show this is a permanent, intensive quality (like saying 'extremely knowledgeable')
Added tanween alif to show this is describing Allah in this sentence
Added ي between second and third root letters with vowels to show this is a strong, permanent quality
Added ًا (an) at the end because it's describing Allah in a 'was' statement - like saying 'Allah was wise'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Adding alif at the beginning makes it causative - showing someone is making something else do the action

steps.m: he causes to enter (subject of يَشَآءُ)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. This word is connected to يَشَآءُ (he wills) in the ayah, showing who He causes to enter.
This is a relative pronoun that refers to Allah in this context, as He is the one who admits into His mercy whom He wills. It connects to the verb يُدْخِلُ (He admits) which comes before it.
Connected the letters and added vowel marks. The middle letter ي changes to ا because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: he wants/wishes (subject of يُدْخِلُ - he admits)
steps.n: Added يَ at the beginning to show it's happening now. Changed the ending to ُ to show it's connected to يُدْخِلُ (he admits) in the ayah. The alif is written long (آ) because it comes before hamza.

steps.m: basic preposition showing location or containment
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that doesn't come from a root. It shows where something is located or contained.
Added kasra (ِ) to show it's connecting two words. In this ayah, it connects 'He admits' (يُدْخِلُ) with 'His mercy' (رَحْمَتِهِ), showing who is being admitted into what.
Changed from root letters to basic noun by adding vowels (fatha and sukun), turning 'show mercy' into 'mercy'

steps.m: a mercy, an instance of mercy
steps.n: Added ة to make it a specific instance of mercy

steps.m: mercy (ready to be connected to something)
steps.n: Changed the ending to connect it with the next part that shows whose mercy it is
Added هِ to show it's His (Allah's) mercy. The small ۦ helps with pronunciation.

steps.m: to act unfairly or harm others

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the word to show who does the action, like changing 'teach' to 'teacher'
Added ين to show we're talking about many people
Added ال to point to specific unfair people, and doubled the ظ letter

steps.m: and the people who act unfairly (who will receive عَذَابًا أَلِيمًۢا)
steps.n: Added و at start to mean 'and', and َ at end to show this word receives the action of أَعَدَّ (He prepared)
Basic past tense verb pattern with 3 fatha vowels (a-a-a) to mean 'he prepared'
Combined the repeated letters with a shadda to show emphasis
Added أَ at the beginning to make it mean 'he prepared (something)' instead of just 'prepared'
Added fatha to show this means 'for/to'

steps.m: for them (referring to الظَّالِمِينَ - the wrongdoers)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show this is 'for them'. This refers back to الظَّالِمِينَ (the wrongdoers) mentioned earlier in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb

Adding an alif between the second and third root letters turns this into a noun that describes the result of the action. This word is what is being prepared in the phrase 'He has prepared for them a painful punishment.'
The ًا ending shows this is the object (thing being prepared) in the sentence
Added ي between second and last root letter with vowels to show this describes something that causes a strong or lasting pain

The ًا shows it's describing the punishment (عَذَابًا) that came before it. This word is an adjective that tells us what kind of punishment it is - it's painful. Since it's describing عَذَابًا which is accusative, this word must also be accusative.
